title: $1,000 in S&P 500 at the 2000 Dot-Com Peak
description: What $1,000 invested in the S&P 500 (via SPY ETF) on January 3, 2000 — near the peak of the dot-com bubble — would be worth today with dividends reinvested. This is the 'worst timing' scenario: buying at a generational market peak.
dividend_note: SPY pays quarterly dividends. DRIP assumed throughout.
max_drawdown_note: The investor suffered a 50% drawdown from the dot-com bust (2000-2002) and then a 57% drawdown from the financial crisis (2007-2009). Despite buying at the worst possible time, they still ended up 440% ahead after 25 years.
worst_timing_note: This is deliberate 'worst timing' scenario. Even buying at a generational market peak, a buy-and-hold investor with dividends reinvested more than 5× their money over 25 years.
educational_note: The powerful lesson here: even the worst possible entry point in modern stock market history (dot-com peak) still produced a 5× return over 25 years with dividends reinvested. Time in the market matters more than timing the market.
